an1.45:1.1: “Suppose there were a pool of water—sullied, turbid, and muddy. A man with good eyesight standing there on the bank would not see shells, gravel, and pebbles, or shoals of fish swimming about and resting.
an1.45:1.3: Because of the sullied nature of the water.
an1.45:1.4: In the same way, that a monk with a sullied mind would know his own benefit, the benefit of others, the benefit of both; that he would realize a superior human state, a truly noble distinction of knowledge & vision: Such a thing is impossible.
an1.45:1.6: Because of the sullied nature of his mind.”
an1.46:1.1: “Suppose there were a pool of water—clear, limpid, and unsullied. A man with good eyesight standing there on the bank would see shells, gravel, & pebbles, and also shoals of fish swimming about and resting.
an1.46:1.3: Because of the unsullied nature of the water.
an1.46:1.4: In the same way, that a monk with an unsullied mind would know his own benefit, the benefit of others, the benefit of both; that he would realize a superior human state, a truly noble distinction of knowledge & vision: Such a thing is possible.
an1.46:1.6: Because of the unsullied nature of his mind.”
